speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 16, 1995. The survivor is Helen Beck, B-E-C-K. The interviewer is Meta Joy Jacoby. The interviewer is staying with us in Delray Beach, Florida, United States of America. In English. This is roll number one.

words.text: The interviewer is staying with us

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Meta Joy Jacoby, J-A-C-O-B-Y, interviewer for the Shoah Foundation. We are speaking to Helen Beck. The date is March 16. We are in Delray Beach, Florida. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Helen Beck--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your--?

speaker: interviewee
text: --born in Poland. And I'm a Holocaust survivor. I was born May 10, 1925. I went to public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth, Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Hela, H-E-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your last-- your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was V-R-Z-E-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city in Poland were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the outskirts of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've already said your birth date. Am I right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which would make you what age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm pushing 70 years old. In a couple months, I will be 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, could you tell me a little about your family and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from a very traditional Jewish family. And I am from a very large family. We were 10 children. I am somewheres in the middle. My parents owned a small business. However, when the war broke out, our lives changed dramatically because of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business, Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father owned a small grain business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, on the outskirts of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, did-- did you have a house? An apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We owned a house. We had the business. And older children were kept busy. In fact, before the war, I had a married brother and two married sisters. And we were still young enough we were going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only finished public school-- seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to Polish public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your relations with the other non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had in school very good relation because I happened to be-- I happened to be a good student. And I was very much respected by the other students, where they were the majority non-Jewish students.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your relations with your teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the teachers we had, they're very good relation. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- what were your parents' relation with the non-Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only through the business, but because also of the neighborhood, my parents were very much involved. There was a very-- a good harmony among the non-Jewish people-- with-- between us and the non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your parents belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only busy with school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. We belonged to a synagogue. And they were very observant Jews, and practicing honesty.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how was Judaism observed in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Observant not only the Jewish holidays, but even kashrut.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in invasion in 1939, how did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the invasion of Poland, our life changed drastically because of all of the rules and regulations. And we were deprived of many things. So we felt immediately when the Nazis occupied Poland because we were, shortly afterwards, dispossessed from our home. The business was taken away. We had to-- we were forced to move in small quarters, mainly in the center of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were deprived of many things. What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, number one, we were deprived in the way they took away our freedom. They took away from us everything, the possessions. But the worst thing was when we were forced to wear the Jewish star-- armband with the Jewish star. And we could not use the public transportation. In fact, we could not even walk on the sidewalk. Many times, we are forced to walk only in the middle of the street. So there were many, many restrictions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I did not comprehend what it was, what war meant. But I felt very strongly what was going gone in the-- in the family life. They understood what war is, whatever. And-- but I learned quickly. It didn't take long where I was affected with the war because in the spring of 1940, during the night, we heard a knock on the door. There were two Nazis and a Jewish policeman. And they almost forcefully broke into the house. And without any explanation, they took my sister and myself away from home, which we never saw the family again. And we were put on open trucks. And they took us away. And they were rounded up other women. And we were brought to a factory. Also, everything outskirts of Krakow. This was a factory very well-known before the war. The [? Pelican ?] factory, which was owned by Jewish people. And the Nazi came over there, took over the factory. And right from the beginning, he started to employ Jewish people. However, I had over there a little bit different experience because when they brought us, and they lined us up in fives, and the managers from different departments, they would come and select people for their department. I was the only Jewish girl taken out from the group and was brought to the Nazi family. And I was over there working as a maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister worked in the garden. Later on, she worked in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about 16 now?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the age of 15, I was already in a forced labor camp. This was a forced labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- where was the house where you were a maid?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the grounds of the factory. It was a big building, a beautiful apartment. They had a maid, and I was brought over there as a helper to the maid. There was also a woman who was a special woman who knew how to cook and bake. But we would basically, the two of us, the Ukrainian girl and myself, we were just cleaning the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to take anything with you when-- when you they took you from your home?

words.text: they took you from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they took us during the night, we barely took a few things in our hand. It's-- you could say next to nothing. And everything by force. And [INAUDIBLE] you just didn't know what to do. You followed orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated in-- in the German household?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the German family, I must say they treated me rather very well. I had no problem over there. And when they were entertaining-- again, they're Nazis. They were entertaining their friends, Nazis. And many times, the lady of the house would take me with pride by the hand into the dining room as to give me a-- a special favor. She would smile, was telling her guests what a cute, Jewish girl she has in her household.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in the household?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was over there 24 hours. I ate. I slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you any contact with the other girls in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have enough freedom. I could move around throughout the camp. And yes, I did have contact. Yes, there are many times. At that time, when you wanted to go to visit or leave this camp, you needed a special permit. And little did I understand, but the people from the camp, they would ask me for a favor since I had direct contact with the director. And I would ask him, and he would issue me without any problem permits. And I was doing this on a regular basis. To me, it meant you could help somebody. I had opportunity. And I was doing this almost constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to help?

speaker: interviewee
text: By issue-- by asking the director to issue for other people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, oh, for others.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the permit for others. Because many times when I came to the office, and he already was smiling. And he would say to me, Helen, where are you traveling now? So he made a joke out of it because he knew very well he saw me 24 hours a day in his household.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the other people use these permits for?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, there still were family living in different places. And they could go visit their families.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was over there for almost a year-- a year and a half. And after a year and a half, the Nazis decided that this is not a secure place because we were watched only by the guards-- with guns and everything. However, they decided to close this-- this factory, this camp. And we were almost the first people brought to Plaszow where they started to build the concentration camp on the Jewish cemetery. So we were, from the beginning, building the roads, building the barracks, and enduring unbelievable horrors in Plaszow because later on, when Amon Goeth came to this camp as a commandant-- And Plaszow will always remain in my mind a place of horror, fear, disease, hunger, and witnessing unbelievable tortures. We saw the Nazis at work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing, you know, could happen. They sent you right away to a barrack. And the next morning, you were right away asked to appear to roll call. And from the roll call, you were assigned to a certain work. And you just followed orders. Either you liked it or not. Either you could physically do it, or you couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: You always, always like to go on, because we learned quickly if you do not obey your orders, so the next thing was a bullet waiting for you. Because in Plaszow, there were daily executions. Sometimes just a few. Sometimes they were mass executions in Plaszow. We who were there, we were forced to watch. We had to witness all kinds of execution, not only by shooting, but by hanging. We remember how Amon Goeth indulged himself when he had the two dogs. And he would take out people from their lineup. And he would order the dogs to tear those people apart. And the dogs were trained. They would jump on those victims. First the clothing. And then they were tearing the flesh apart like nothing. And Amon Goeth would stay next to, watching like almost it was entertainment for him. Yet with the pain and everything, we couldn't do nothing. We were forced to watch that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Plaszow working all kinds of works. I mean all kinds, and several different work. I was in the beginning work-- working in the quarry. I was working late on building the roads, the barracks. Then we were working in a barrack making necklaces for the Nazi soldiers. And they were sending out these on the [? Tour ?] [? de ?] France. The last job in Plaszow for me was in the laundry. And I was on the night shift. According to all what we know, that Amon Goeth passed by the barrack, and he noticed that we were not working too hard. The following morning, our work in the laundry was dissolved. And we were working over there again for a few days by building the roads. At that time, this was already March 13, 1943 when the Krakow ghetto was liquidating. This is a date for me is gonna live forever because during the liquidation of Krakow ghetto, my parents-- both parents-- were murdered during the liquidation. And other members of the family, but mainly my parents were murdered. And they were buried, which during the liquidation, there were thousands of people killed. And the bodies were brought to Plaszow and buried in a mass grave. A few days later after the liquidation of Krakow ghetto, they rounded up a group of women. Since this was 1943-- it started in '42-- there were daily selections and daily deportations. When they rounded us up, we were almost certain that we would be sent away from Plaszow somewheres to a death camp. Little did we know that we were the lucky people because other groups, exactly what had happened. They were rounded up, put on the-- into the cattle cars, and they were sent away to the death camps. We were very fortunate. And I believe in miracles because this was like a miracle. Our group was rounded up, put on open trucks, and we were sent away, sent out from Plaszow. And we were brought to Schindler's factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Plaszow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'd just like you to speak a little bit more in detail. What was-- where were you-- you housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, from the beginning, we were-- I'll-- not a large group-- we were-- women and men were separated. We were in the barracks for women. And the barracks were very huge. And we had three-tired bunk beds. And we slept almost like sardines next one to each other because it was crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: How you survive living in a-- in a barrack like that? It is-- You cannot describe because not only would we suffered from the Nazis hand, but nature played a trick on us, too. Because this was simply of lack of sanitation. And we discovered-- I never had seen, never knew about, there are certain bugs. And in the wood in the barracks, and they were eating us alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could not go out, stay outside, because you were always chased by the Nazis. You had to remain in those barracks. So we experienced all kind of horrors, all shape, all color. It was like living through hell. And yet we had no choice. And we, in spite of everything, we were hoping that maybe the war is not gonna last long. And somehow, we will again enjoy freedom and return to a normal life. However, little did we know what was waiting for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- can you describe the food in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow? The food you could barely survive. And you could see how people were getting from the malnutrition skin and bones. And some people were dying like I said, from hunger, from disease. Not single, but by the-- many people. Whole groups of people. You cannot survive. Like one lady we knew, and I remember her very well, she was a medic before the war. When we started to complain like almost we were ready to give up. So she would sometimes come up and give us courage to go on. And she said, you cannot die from hunger because, she says, I will tell you something. You can survive 30 days without food. You can survive three days without water. Nothing will happen. However, I want you to think and realize you cannot survive three minutes without hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for us and later on, and from the beginning, I still say like the Nazis stripped us but from everything. The only thing what the Nazis did not succeed, they could not take away from us hope and faith. Because of hope and faith, we were going on from day to day. Did you hold on to your faith? Your faith in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never gave up faith, never gave up hope. We became aware if you give up hope, and you give up faith, there's nothing left. And in order to survive, we learned later on, you needed something-- something bigger. You needed a source of-- of courage, a source of faith. So this was the belief. And that's what carried us through.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were sanitary conditions in these barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the saddest-- not only the sad, but the most horrible part from living in those concentration camps. Because the sanitary conditions, they were much worse than hunger. The only thing what we learned later on, it was almost like a miracle. Because the Nazis did put in something in our food. And the periods disappeared like in one day. So this was at least one thing we didn't have to deal with. The sanitary, they were non-existent. And even you want it or not, then, you know, the lice were crawling like-- It's just unbelievable, the sanitary conditions. But I remember, and I don't know exactly how and why, we were always fighting. For instance, like we would get a little black coffee for breakfast. And we were constantly hungry. We would save a little bit of the black coffee and wash our faces. And we were trying to remain human as much as possible in spite what the Nazis tried dehumanize us, humiliate us constantly. They degraded us so terribly that you only wondered how a civilized nation can do things like that to others. This bugged us very much. And yet, they were always considered, the Nazis, Germany was more civilized country than any countries in Europe. And how low they went. How they could do things to others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any heat in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks were slightly heated. But, you know, we were over-crowded. So probably from the heat of the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any medical care?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, not as I know-- I am positive. Yes, there was a hospital. Yes, there was something to know that you could go for some help. Thank god I didn't go. There were many times I could have needed. But you were avoiding it because this was a danger. Once your reported over there in the Krankenstube, in the hospital, so you were already like a marked person almost. They would keep you for the day or whatever. The next morning, they would round up a group of people, bring you on the hill, and put you in front of a pit. And the Nazis would shoot you like nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there both male and female SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I am almost embarrassed to say because being a woman, the SS women were much worse. They were more vicious than the SS people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the female SS with the female prisoners? Or-- or was-- didn't matter--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were all-- you know, they had to feed them. They-- they were all over the camp. They had their own place of living. And for them, you know, it was only like a job to be in charge of the people in camp. However, how they did their job. They were using unbelievable tortures. For them, this was like entertainment. No question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you go into some detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: The details-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we would see an SS woman coming closer where we were working, if you could crawl under the ground in a mouse hole some way, you would gladly do it. You couldn't. But again, the same thing. You know, they would take you out from the lineup or from your work of place, from the place where you were working. They were simply torturing you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would take you in front. Just to give you an example, if you-- because you had to stay straight in front of them. If you looked directly in their eyes, they would smack you on the face. And they would ask you-- but in German, it sounds much better-- why do you look so funny at me? And when you were trying to avoid not looking at them, you were trying to look somewheres away, they would kick you and hit you again. How come you're not looking at me because I'm talking? You know, those are certain things what they created. And they were torturing people. You then mention that you were notified and taken out by truck. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this was not so nice. It sounds much better, the way you use the word, notify.

speaker: interviewer
text: I agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just rounding up people at random. And they rounded up a group of women. We were put on the trucks, not telling us what's going to happen, what-- what is the purpose of putting us on the trucks. However, we were very fortunate because we were brought to Schindler's factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know exactly, but there were over 100, 150. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: All women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just women that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you realize?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Schindler's factory. When we entered the factory, of course, the first were the Nazi guards. Not welcoming, you know. They were there the first. But among the Nazi guards, there was a man, very tall, very good looking, beautifully dressed. Not knowing, this was Oskar Schindler waiting for us in there when we were entering the factory. When we entered the factory, right away that time there was-- Schindler made sure. Because on the grounds of the factory, he made like a sub-camp with everything what the Nazis required with the barbed wires, the watchtowers. And the Nazi men and Nazi women were over there. So when we entered the factory, we walked through the courtyard. And we were brought to our barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sent to the barracks. And there was a roll call. And there was some managers, which I was not aware. And we were assigned next morning to work in the factory. And as you know-- What was the barracks like, Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, barracks are barracks. They're the bunk beds. I was only fortunate because I was in a small barrack at that time, which they didn't have three tiers. But they were two tiers, which made a big difference. But later on if more people came to Schindler, they built a huge barrack with this same style like in Plaszow. Very crowded. Three tiers. And again, we were assigned to work in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were sanitary conditions any better?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Schindler's factory, definitely. There was a barrack, what we called a latrine. There were, you know, running water. And there were bathrooms. Of course, they were not, you know, like you would expect-- English baths and whatever. But there was running water. Even it was cold, you could stay clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was soap?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.
